articles.title: ICSC 2026: Inflation, Trampoline Parks and the Fate of Luxury
articles.narrative: This article summarizes key takeaways from the ICSC conference, highlighting that retail real estate is experiencing a strong resurgence in leasing and investment, driven by a long period of underdevelopment and demolition of older centers. However, this strength is not yet fully reflected in Net Operating Income (NOI). Inflation and evolving consumer behavior are major concerns, with shoppers increasingly seeking value and convenience, leading to more frequent visits and a boost for discount stores. The industry is also focused on tenant remixing in shopping centers, a re-evaluation of luxury brand strategies, and the surprising growth of entertainment retail, including trampoline parks. The potential impact of artificial intelligence on real estate data and analytics is also a significant topic of discussion.

people.name: Meghann Martindale
people.title: Principal and Director of Retail Market Intelligence

articles.title: Dutch Investor Pays $110M For New Rental in Aventura, Fla.
articles.narrative: Dutch investor Breevest has acquired Avida Aventura, a newly constructed 266-unit multifamily property in Aventura, FL, for $110 million. The seller, Fifield Companies, completed the eight-story building in 2024 after securing a $112 million construction loan from Ladder Capital in 2021. This transaction marks Breevest's entry into the South Florida market, with BDT & MSD Partners providing $85 million in acquisition financing.

data_points.notable_features: Located across the street from the 1.8 million-square-foot Aventura Mall.
data_points.project_notes: The 1.8-acre site was previously acquired by Fifield Companies for $12 million in 2021. Ladder Capital provided a $112 million construction loan in 2021.

articles.market_intelligence: South Florida's multifamily investment market has seen increased activity, despite a slowdown in rent growth since the pandemic's peak. Recent transactions include the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints' real estate arm acquiring a 456-unit complex in Boca Raton for $240 million, and LeFrak Organization purchasing a waterfront rental tower in Fort Lauderdale for $180 million.

articles.title: Peachtree Group Provides $32M Construction Loan for Alabama Hotel
articles.narrative: Peachtree Group has provided a $32.36 million construction loan to Spandrel Development Partners for the development of a 154-room AC Hotel by Marriott in Huntsville, AL. The hotel will be located within the Bridge Street Town Centre, a part of Cummings Research Park. The project is expected to benefit from strong demand drivers in the area, including government and defense-related activities, as well as significant economic and employment growth over the past decade.

data_points.notable_features: Located within Bridge Street Town Centre, close to U.S. Space & Rocket Center, University of Alabama-Huntsville, and Redstone Arsenal.
data_points.project_notes: The 4.67-acre site was acquired by Spandrel Development Partners in September 2025.

articles.market_intelligence: Huntsville, AL has experienced 23% employment growth over the past decade. Recent economic boosts include Blue Origin's expansion in Cummings Research Park and Eli Lilly and Company's new $6 billion manufacturing facility. The U.S. Space Command is also developing a permanent headquarters at Redstone Arsenal.

articles.title: NYC Developers File Plans for 21M SF of New Construction in Q1: REBNY
articles.narrative: New York City developers filed plans for 577 new buildings in Q1 2026, totaling 21.2 million square feet, a 74% increase year-over-year. The majority of these filings, 75% or 15.5 million square feet, were for multifamily projects, proposing 16,815 new units across 281 buildings. This surge in activity is seen as encouraging for the city's economy and housing shortage, especially following the expiration of the 421a tax abatement program in 2022 and the subsequent decline in housing filings. Despite a national downturn in multifamily construction starts, New York City maintains a robust construction pipeline.

data_points.notable_features: Filings include a mix of residential, commercial, hospitality, and a dentition facility project.
data_points.project_notes: The increase in filings follows a decline after the 421a tax incentive program expired in 2022; the new 485x program has faced criticism.

companies_people.label: OTHER
companies_people.firm_name: Real Estate Board of New York (REBNY)

people.name: Basha Gerhards
people.title: Executive Vice President of Public Policy

articles.market_intelligence: The national multifamily market saw construction starts hit a low in Q1 2026, reaching 55,000 units nationwide, a 73% decrease from the early 2022 peak. In contrast, New York City had 43,000 units under construction at the start of the year, outperforming markets like Dallas-Fort Worth (31,000 units).

articles.title: The Definition of ‘Jobs’ in the Data Center Economy Needs to Shift
articles.narrative: The author argues that the data center industry needs to redefine how it measures and communicates its job creation impact to local communities. Instead of focusing on the limited number of permanent, on-site jobs, the industry should emphasize the broader ecosystem of employment it generates, including construction, infrastructure, maintenance, and specialized technical roles. The core argument is that data center projects should be evaluated not by a fixed job count, but by their ability to create durable workforce pipelines that connect local residents to long-term opportunities in the digital and energy economy. This shift requires binding workforce development partnerships with local educational institutions and a more standardized, measurable approach to assessing long-term employment outcomes, especially when projects receive public incentives.

companies_people.label: OWNER
companies_people.firm_name: New York University's Schack Institute of Real Estate

people.name: Suhail Y Tayeb
people.title: Clinical Assistant Professor and Director of the Center for the Sustainable Built Environment

articles.title: Provident Industrial Completes 161,408 SF Project in Arlington
articles.narrative: Provident Industrial has completed the A20 Logistics Center, a 161,408-square-foot industrial project in Arlington, designed to accommodate up to four tenants. The development includes infrastructure improvements such as an extended city roadway and a new traffic signal at the property entrance, enhancing accessibility and functionality for future occupants.

data_points.notable_features: Designed to accommodate up to four tenants with knockout panels and doors for central entrance configuration.
data_points.project_notes: Infrastructure improvements included an extended city roadway and a new traffic signal at the property entrance.

articles.title: Three Tenants Sign Office Leases Totaling 36,548 SF at Frisco Station
articles.narrative: Three new tenants have signed office leases totaling 36,548 square feet at Offices Three within Frisco Station, a 242-acre mixed-use development north of Dallas. The new leases include an architecture and engineering firm, a cement manufacturer, and a financial advisory group, adding to the diverse tenant mix at the property.

articles.title: Finial Group Negotiates 14,100 SF Industrial Lease Renewal in Northwest Houston
articles.narrative: Finial Group has successfully negotiated a 14,100-square-foot industrial lease renewal in northwest Houston. The tenant, a company serving the energy industry, has occupied the space for over a decade, underscoring the property's long-term appeal and strategic location.

articles.title: Newman Commercial Buys 7,100 SF Retail Strip Center in The Woodlands, Texas
articles.narrative: Newman Commercial Real Estate has acquired Grogan's Forest Center, a 7,100-square-foot retail strip center in The Woodlands, Texas. The off-market transaction involved a property with a strong tenant mix, including various food and service establishments. The seller and the specific sales price were not disclosed.

data_points.project_notes: Deal traded off-market.

articles.title: Cronheim Arranges $75M in Financing for Retail Power Center in Middletown, New York
articles.narrative: Cronheim Mortgage has arranged $75 million in financing for Orange Plaza, an 811,272-square-foot retail power center in Middletown, New York. The seven-year loan, featuring a 30-year amortization schedule, was secured through an undisclosed life insurance company on behalf of National Realty & Development. The property was 94 percent leased at the time of the transaction, boasting a strong tenant mix.

data_points.project_notes: Seven-year loan with a 30-year amortization schedule.

articles.title: JLL closes sale of 244-room hotel in Houston’s CITYCENTRE district
articles.narrative: JLL's Hotels & Hospitality Group has completed the sale of The Moran Hotel CITYCENTRE, a 244-room lifestyle hotel located within Houston's 47-acre CITYCENTRE mixed-use district. Midway sold the asset to MetroNational in a joint venture acquisition. The hotel, which underwent a multimillion-dollar renovation in 2024, is a focal point of the walkable CITYCENTRE development and includes 22 condominium units and ground-floor retail space.

data_points.notable_features: Located within the 47-acre CITYCENTRE mixed-use district, which includes over 400,000 SF of retail, 1,500+ multifamily units, and 1.5M SF of Class A office space. The hotel completed a multimillion-dollar renovation in 2024 and includes 15,750 SF of meeting space, an outdoor pool, and a fitness center. It is situated at the intersection of Interstate 10 and Beltway 8.
data_points.project_notes: The property opened in 2009 as Hotel Sorella before rebranding as the Moran in 2020. The offering included 22 condominium units on floors 11-12 and 14,645 SF of ground floor retail.

articles.market_intelligence: CITYCENTRE ranks as a top 10 lifestyle retail center statewide, attracting over 4.6 million visitors annually. The location provides access to more than two million residents within 20 minutes and 243,000 employees within a five-mile radius.

articles.title: Haven Housing acquires 309-unit multifamily community in Kyle
articles.narrative: Haven Housing, the multifamily and affordable housing platform of BH Properties, has acquired Hays Park Apartments, a 309-unit garden-style multifamily community in Kyle, Texas. This acquisition expands Haven Housing's portfolio in a rapidly growing market.

data_points.notable_features: Garden-style multifamily community.

articles.title: Younger Partners negotiates sale of 182 acres in Howe
articles.narrative: Younger Partners brokered the sale of the 182-acre Hightower tract in Howe, Texas, from the Hightower family to Cherukuru Investments at Howe LLC. The property, located at the northeast corner of FM 902 and Bennett Road, is positioned for a phased mixed-use development with residential and retail components, capitalizing on the rapid growth along the U.S. 75 corridor. The buyer plans to begin with infrastructure improvements, followed by residential and commercial construction over the next five to ten years.

data_points.notable_features: Located outside Howe city limits, partially within Sherman and Howe ETJ, less than two miles from U.S. 75.
data_points.project_notes: The land has been owned by the Hightower family for approximately 140 years. Potential plans include a phased mixed-use development with residential and retail components.

articles.title: The Missner Group acquires three industrial properties in Northwest Indiana, Indianapolis markets
articles.narrative: The Missner Group has significantly expanded its industrial portfolio by acquiring three Class-A industrial properties totaling over 840,000 square feet across Northwest Indiana and the Indianapolis market. This strategic acquisition nearly doubles the company's industrial holdings to more than 2.2 million square feet. The properties, which include a two-building portfolio in Merrillville and a logistics facility in Whitestown, are fully leased to a strong tenant mix, underscoring their appeal in active distribution corridors.

data_points.notable_features: The Merrillville properties offer direct access to I-65, I-80/I-94, and I-90. The Whitestown facility features 32-foot clear heights, ESFR sprinklers, LED warehouse lighting, 30 dock doors, and trailer parking.
data_points.project_notes: The Merrillville portfolio was built in 2021 and 2022. The Whitestown property was completed in 2023.

articles.title: Starwood lending arm trying to flip Back Bay office
articles.narrative: LNR Partners, the lending arm of Starwood Property Trust, is marketing the Park Square Building in Boston's Back Bay for sale, just two months after acquiring it at auction for $95 million. The 503,000-square-foot office property is being pitched with significant redevelopment potential, including a possible mixed-use conversion. The building is currently 35.8 percent leased with a weighted average remaining lease term of less than four years, making it an attractive candidate for repositioning given the city's incentives for office-to-residential conversions.

data_points.notable_features: 11-story building with significant redevelopment potential, including a transformative mixed-use redevelopment.
data_points.project_notes: LNR Partners acquired the property in March for $95 million after it was in distress; a $160 million loan moved to special servicing in 2024. The previous owner, Capital Properties, purchased the building in 1997 for $57 million.

articles.market_intelligence: Office-to-residential conversions are a growing trend in the Boston office market, supported by a city program that offers expedited project approvals and up to 75 percent in tax abatements. The Boston Planning & Development Agency recently approved four such conversions.

articles.title: ZOM’s Arizona debut price tag: $323M in legal penalties
articles.narrative: A Maricopa County jury ordered ZOM Living to pay $323 million in damages to the now-defunct Gray Development Group, finding ZOM liable for breaching a joint venture agreement. Gray Development Group accused ZOM of using its proprietary information, market intelligence, and relationships to launch its first multifamily projects in Arizona, effectively freezing Gray out of a planned $1.4 billion development pipeline. The dispute centered on four apartment developments in Phoenix and Scottsdale that began construction last year and are slated for completion next year.

articles.title: Ex-Washington Commanders owner relists massive estate for $10M off
articles.narrative: Former Washington Commanders owner Dan Snyder and his wife Tanya have relisted their River View estate in Alexandria, Virginia, for $49.9 million, a $10 million reduction from its initial asking price in June 2024. The Snyders acquired the 16.5-acre property in October 2021 for $48 million, which remains the most expensive residential sale in the Washington, D.C. area's history. The price adjustment reflects a strategic move to attract a buyer as the couple continues to divest their real estate holdings in the region following the sale of their football team in 2023.

data_points.notable_features: The property includes a 16,000-square-foot main house with eight bedrooms, a library, game room, home office, fitness center, spa, 15-seat home theater, wine cellar, and a three-car garage. The grounds feature a 2,600-square-foot guest house, a carriage house, a private dock, a boxwood garden, and 400 feet of Potomac River frontage.
data_points.project_notes: The Snyders previously acquired the estate in October 2021 for $48 million. The initial listing price in June 2024 was $60 million.

articles.title: Floyd Mayweather hits Jona Rechnitz with fraud lawsuit, alleges diversion of $175M
articles.narrative: Floyd Mayweather Jr. has filed a lawsuit against his former investment manager, Jona Rechnitz, alleging a multi-year fraud scheme that diverted $175 million in cash, jewelry, and real estate funds. The lawsuit claims Rechnitz misrepresented Mayweather's acquisition of a 1,000-unit affordable housing portfolio in New York City, where Mayweather allegedly received only a 5% stake in one LLC instead of the entire portfolio. Additionally, Rechnitz is accused of diverting $15 million from a settlement with SL Green and $8.8 million in loan proceeds from Hankey Capital, secured by four of Mayweather's properties, to an associate's company.

data_points.project_notes: Lawsuit alleges misrepresentation of a 1,000-unit affordable housing portfolio acquisition, where the plaintiff claims to have received only a 5% stake in one LLC.

articles.title: Sherwin Belkin, prominent New York landlord-side attorney, to retire
articles.narrative: Sherwin Belkin, a co-founding partner at Belkin Burden Goldman and a prominent New York landlord-side attorney, is set to retire in October after a 50-year legal career. Belkin was a key figure in rent-stabilization law, helping landlords deregulate units and navigate the city's evolving housing regulations. He noted that the environment for property owners, managers, and developers has become increasingly challenging due to the volume of regulations, particularly for small and mid-sized owners. His work shifted significantly after the 2019 Housing Stability and Tenant Protection Act, moving from tenant buyouts and deregulation to due diligence for buyers and sellers to assess potential overcharge liabilities.

articles.title: Modified COPA nears passage despite real estate pushback
articles.narrative: A revised version of New York City's Community Opportunity to Purchase Act (COPA) is nearing passage, despite continued skepticism from the real estate industry. The bill, which grants city-certified nonprofits or joint ventures a right of first refusal on certain distressed multifamily buildings, includes concessions such as a shortened timeline for offers and stricter eligibility criteria for properties. While the current mayor supports the bill, real estate professionals argue that the changes are marginal and will still complicate financing, deter investors, and depress sale prices for affected properties.

data_points.notable_features: Applies to buildings with four or more units that meet specific distress criteria, including an average of three or more violations per unit or inclusion in the city's Alternative Enforcement Program.
data_points.project_notes: The revised COPA bill shortens the timeline for potential buyers to express interest (20 days) and submit an offer (70 days), with capped extensions. It also applies to buildings with affordability restrictions expiring in two years, but only for properties with no more than 100 units and exempts those with 421a tax breaks. An estimated 335 city buildings out of 51,000 sold in 2025 would have been affected by the current criteria.

companies_people.label: OTHER
companies_people.firm_name: Real Estate Board of New York

articles.title: Cain, Alchemy-ABR score $321M refi for Billionaires’ Row office tower
articles.narrative: Cain and Alchemy-ABR Investment Partners secured a $321 million refinancing package for their recently completed 30-story office tower at 125 West 57th Street in New York City. The financing, provided by JPMorgan Chase and Hudson Bay Capital, replaces a $250 million construction loan. The Class A tower, built on spec, has attracted strong tenant interest with recent leases exceeding $300 per square foot, reflecting the robust demand for trophy office space in Manhattan's Plaza District.

data_points.address: 125 West 57th Street, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: Class A tower with Central Park views and 7,000 square feet of retail space, designed by FXCollaborative.
data_points.project_notes: The joint venture acquired the property in 2021 for $130 million. Calvary Baptist Church retained ownership of its 180-foot-high base portion of the building.

articles.market_intelligence: Manhattan recorded 313 leases at $100 per square foot or higher last year, with 28 deals exceeding $200 per square foot. A lease at 9 West 57th Street in April set a borough record for office rents at $327.50 per square foot.

articles.title: Meet Poorvesh Thakkar, the Dallas owner Safehold is trying to eject from a Manhattan tower
articles.narrative: Safehold, the ground lessor of the 135 West 50th Street office tower in Midtown Manhattan, has filed a lawsuit to eject Thakkar Developers from the property. Safehold alleges that the Dallas-based developer, led by Poorvesh Thakkar, failed to meet its obligations under a long-term ground lease, resulting in nearly $28 million in unpaid property taxes, interest, and penalties. Thakkar Developers had acquired the ground lease for the nearly 1 million-square-foot building for $8.5 million and announced plans for an office-to-residential conversion. This lawsuit adds to a series of legal challenges for Thakkar, including recent fraud charges from the SEC and multiple lawsuits related to his Dallas-area projects.

data_points.address: 135 West 50th Street, New York City, NY
data_points.project_notes: The reported sale price of $8.5 million was for the ground lease, not the fee simple interest in the property. The developer plans an office-to-residential conversion.

articles.title: Seller tied to investor Randall Smith sells Palm Beach mansion for $93M
articles.narrative: A company linked to Randall Smith of Alden Global Capital sold a newly constructed, 13,000-square-foot mansion on Everglades Island in Palm Beach for $93.3 million. The 1-acre waterfront property was purchased by a trust managed by attorney Mark Meland, who secured a $70 million mortgage from Bank of America. This transaction represents the second-priciest lakefront home sale in Palm Beach without ocean frontage.

data_points.notable_features: Eight-bedroom house with 1,400 square feet of covered outdoor living space, designed in an art deco style with nautical details, located on Everglades Island with 371 feet of waterfront.
data_points.project_notes: The seller previously acquired the lot for $39.5 million in 2021. The home's design was approved by the town's architectural commission in 2022.

articles.title: Forest Development wins bid to build at Riviera Beach’s Marina Village
articles.narrative: Forest Development has been selected by Riviera Beach city council members to develop a 12-acre waterfront site at Marina Village. The proposed $556.1 million phased development will include a Marriott Autograph Collection hotel, a convention center, a condominium with boat storage, restaurant/office buildings, a revamped park, and a culinary school. The developer must finalize an agreement with the city in the coming months, with project funding expected to come from existing loans and a commitment to contribute to a community benefit fund.

data_points.notable_features: Proposed development includes a 20-story Marriott Autograph Collection hotel, a possible 10-story convention center, a 20-story condo with boat storage, and a culinary school run by celebrity chef Richard Burke.
data_points.project_notes: The project is part of an 80-acre assemblage that city officials have been trying to redevelop for over a decade. Forest Development plans to contribute $100,000 annually to a community benefit fund. The development is projected to generate $2.9 million annually in city revenue and create 1,000 permanent jobs. The bid process was preceded by an FBI subpoena for Marina Village documents from city officials.

articles.title: Brokers, developers use the World Cup to pitch foreign buyers. Here’s where they’re coming from
articles.narrative: The World Cup is serving as a catalyst for condo sales to foreign buyers in South Florida, particularly from Latin America, at a time when the residential market is experiencing a slowdown. Brokers and developers are leveraging the event to attract international investors, with some offering incentives like World Cup tickets and airline miles to sweeten deals. While not the sole driver, the event is accelerating purchasing decisions, especially for short-term rental-friendly condos that help buyers offset expenses. The weak dollar is also contributing to the increased international demand, with strong buyer pools from countries like Brazil, Colombia, Argentina, and Mexico, as well as continued interest from the United Kingdom, Spain, and Germany.

data_points.notable_features: Short-term rental friendly condo project.
data_points.project_notes: Developers offered World Cup tickets and a brokerage partnership with Latam Airlines offered airline miles as incentives for buyers.

articles.market_intelligence: The World Cup is being used by brokers and developers to attract foreign buyers to Miami, similar to other major events like Art Basel, Formula 1, and the Miami Open. Miami is hosting seven FIFA World Cup 2026 matches at Hard Rock Stadium in Miami Gardens. The weakness of the dollar has helped fuel an uptick in international demand for real estate. Buyers from Colombia, Mexico, Argentina, and Brazil were the top countries represented by foreign purchases in South Florida from January 2024 through October 2025. Interest also continues from the United Kingdom, Spain, and Germany.

articles.title: Related Group taps Live Local for Hollywood beachfront project, sidestepping county land-use ruling
“Subpar construction”: Amrit Ocean Resort owners sue developer, construction team over alleged defects
articles.narrative: The condo association for Amrit Ocean Resort has filed a lawsuit against the developer, Wellness Resorts and Wellness Resorts & Spa, and the construction team, including Optimal Construction, Paramount Consulting & Engineering, S&E Architects, and CAP Government. The suit alleges pervasive construction defects, including roofing failures, corrosion in mechanical systems, structural issues, and window failures, which became evident after a recent rain event. This legal action follows the developer's turnover of condo association control to unit owners and adds to existing disputes regarding the property's classification and eligibility for homestead tax exemptions.

data_points.notable_features: Beachfront two-tower project on Singer Island.
data_points.project_notes: Development was completed 15 years after initial conception. The building does not yet have a final certificate of occupancy.

companies_people.label: OWNER
companies_people.firm_name: Amrit Ocean Resort and Residences Condo Association

articles.title: Judge orders Chicago MLS to restore Zillow access to listings
articles.narrative: A federal judge has ordered Midwest Real Estate Data (MRED), the Multiple Listing Service operator for northern Illinois, to immediately restore Zillow's access to its Chicagoland listing feeds. This ruling grants a temporary restraining order, reinstating approximately 43,000 listings to Zillow's platform and temporarily halting a dispute with MRED and brokerage Compass. The conflict arose after MRED cut Zillow's data feed, allegedly in response to Zillow's "Listing Access Standards" which restrict privately marketed listings, a practice heavily utilized by Compass.

articles.title: Chicago brokerages navigate Zillow access after listing purge
articles.narrative: More than half of Chicago-area home listings vanished from Zillow after Midwest Real Estate Data (MRED), the MLS operator for northern Illinois, cut off its listing feed. MRED cited Zillow's refusal to display privately marketed listings as a violation of MLS rules. Zillow has sued MRED and Compass, alleging collusion to pressure Zillow into displaying these listings. While some brokerages like Baird & Warner and Keller Williams OneChicago have established direct feeds to Zillow to maintain visibility, Compass International Holdings and its brands have ceased their direct feeds, embracing the strategy of offering exclusive listings not found on Zillow. Brokers express mixed feelings about the impact, with some seeing an advantage in increased prominence for their listings on Zillow, while others are concerned about lead generation.

articles.title: Interim Chicago Housing Authority head Matthew Brewer considering mayoral run
articles.narrative: Matthew Brewer, the interim operating chairman of the Chicago Housing Authority (CHA), is reportedly considering a mayoral run in Chicago. Brewer has been engaging with prominent business figures, including the board of One Future Illinois, a non-profit that has previously clashed with incumbent Mayor Brandon Johnson over corporate and real estate transfer taxes. This potential political move comes amidst an ongoing power struggle between Brewer and Mayor Johnson regarding the leadership of the CHA, an agency with a $1.4 billion operating budget overseeing over 65,000 households.

companies_people.label: OWNER
companies_people.firm_name: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development

articles.title: Israeli manufacturer of food delivery drones opens facility in north Dallas suburb
articles.narrative: Flytrex Aviation, an Israeli drone manufacturer, has established an 8,000-square-foot production facility in Pilot Point, Texas, capable of producing approximately 1,000 drones annually. This expansion supports the company's goal to rapidly scale its drone food delivery services, with plans to open 60 new delivery sites across the Dallas-Fort Worth Metroplex by 2027. The company aims to grow its local team from 20 to 50 employees, leveraging the DFW area's role as an FAA model for future drone regulations.

data_points.notable_features: Facility capable of producing approximately 1,000 drones per year.
data_points.project_notes: Company plans to expand its team from 20 to 50 employees.

articles.market_intelligence: The Dallas-Fort Worth Metroplex is being used as a model by the Federal Aviation Administration to set rules for future drone use nationwide. Pilot Point is experiencing rapid growth, with Green Brick Partners acquiring 557 acres in 2024 for 2,200 homes, and the Pilot Point City Council approving 1,331 homes on 350+ acres along FM 1385 in February.

articles.title: Texas Capital Bank moving into Stonelake’s 415 Colorado in Austin
articles.narrative: Texas Capital Bank has signed a lease for the entire 17th floor and a portion of the 16th floor at 415 Colorado, a 47-story mixed-use tower in downtown Austin. The bank will relocate its San Jacinto Center branch to this new location. The building, owned by Stonelake Capital Partners, is now 50% leased for its office component, with its residential units at 86% occupancy.

data_points.notable_features: Mixed-use tower with 110,000 square feet of office space and 328 luxury apartment units.

articles.market_intelligence: Net absorption of office space in Austin was negative in 2025, indicating a slowdown after a period of high demand from tech companies relocating during the pandemic.

articles.title: Austin in line for $30M of libertarian development devoted to Ayn Rand
articles.narrative: The Ayn Rand Museum and Archives Foundation, an arm of the Ayn Rand Institute, is developing a two-story, 27,144-square-foot Ayn Rand Center in Austin, TX. The $30 million project, located near the University of Texas at Austin campus, will feature a library, exhibition room, offices, and higher education classroom spaces. Construction is slated to begin in July and conclude in October 2028, marking the institute's and foundation's migration to Austin.

data_points.notable_features: Located near the University of Texas at Austin and the University of Austin, featuring a library, exhibition room, office, and higher education classroom spaces.
data_points.project_notes: The foundation previously acquired a 0.4-acre lot at 1900 San Gabriel Street in 2023 and a neighboring 0.2-acre lot at 1904 San Gabriel Street in 2024 for undisclosed prices.

companies_people.label: DEVELOPER/SPONSOR
companies_people.firm_name: Ayn Rand Museum and Archives Foundation

articles.title: H-E-B to close Austin store in move that spotlights company’s evolution into real estate empire
articles.narrative: H-E-B is closing its Spicewood Springs Road store in Austin, TX, by July 31 as its lease expires. This closure underscores the company's strategic shift from a traditional grocer to a significant real estate owner and developer, as it now owns the land and buildings for its modern, larger stores. The company is actively expanding its footprint across Texas, particularly in high-growth areas like Dallas-Fort Worth, by acquiring substantial acreage and developing new, larger format stores.

data_points.notable_features: 80s-era store, significantly smaller than modern H-E-B locations.
data_points.project_notes: Store is closing due to lease expiration.

articles.title: Tech firm snags foreclosed San Jose office building at 24% discount
articles.narrative: A tech firm, Rocket EMS, acquired a foreclosed 78,200-square-foot research and office building at 5729 Fontanoso Way in San Jose, CA, for $18.8 million. The property was sold by asset management firm Evergreen Advantage, which had taken possession of the building after it went into default and foreclosure. The sale price represents a significant discount from its 2024 appraised value of $24.5 million, highlighting the distress in the Bay Area commercial real estate market.

data_points.notable_features: Acquired out of foreclosure at a 23.5% discount from its 2024 value.
data_points.project_notes: Building fell into default in 2023 and sat vacant for years, undergoing bankruptcy before lender seizure. A life sciences firm had leased the building in 2021 but never moved in.

articles.market_intelligence: Other recent office transactions in San Jose include BGO's acquisition of Rio Tech Office Park for $164.3 million (a slight discount from its 2021 price) and Paceline Investors' purchase of 2581 Junction Avenue for $12.2 million, a 61.9% discount from its 2018 sale price.

articles.title: Sutter Health to build tallest tower in Emeryville with updated hospital plan
articles.narrative: Sutter Health has unveiled an updated, larger vision for its planned Emeryville Medical Center, a $1 billion project that will feature a 17-story, 780,000-square-foot hospital with 325 beds. This development, which will become Emeryville's tallest building, is part of Sutter's strategy to transform the 12-acre former Emery Yards biotech campus into a regional medical hub. The project is intended to replace the aging Alta Bates Summit Medical Center campus in Berkeley, which is slated to close in 2030 due to seismic standards. Construction is expected to begin in 2029 with an opening targeted for 2033.

data_points.notable_features: Will become Emeryville's tallest building, surpassing the 310-foot Pacific Park Plaza.
data_points.project_notes: The 12-acre site was previously acquired from BioMed Realty for $450 million last year. The project includes converting existing buildings at 5555 Hollis Street and 5300 Chiron Way into medical office and ambulatory care facilities, plus a future 55,000-square-foot building. It is expected to replace Sutter's Alta Bates Summit Medical Center campus in Berkeley, which closes in 2030.

articles.market_intelligence: Health care employment in the East Bay has increased by 25% since 2020. The softening life sciences real estate market in Emeryville could see some absorption from this project.

articles.title: Sprawling Sonoma County estate flirts with local price record
articles.narrative: Ghost Donkey Ranch, a 227-acre estate in Knights Valley, Sonoma County, is being listed for $35 million. The property, which includes a 4,200-square-foot main house and two guest houses, is a working cattle and agricultural ranch. The seller, Adam Gordon, managing partner of Wildflower, acquired the land in 2011 and developed the estate, which also serves as the base for his Wagyu beef company.

data_points.notable_features: Working cattle and agricultural ranch, free of pesticides, fertilizers, or chemical agents. Includes a four-car garage and a 10-car show garage.
data_points.project_notes: Seller Adam Gordon purchased the land in 2011 with his then-wife Kristina O'Neal. The property was restored from overgrazed and undeveloped land.

articles.market_intelligence: The priciest home sale ever recorded in Sonoma County was $30 million in 2010.

articles.title: Investment Firm Acquires SoHo Mixed-Use for $43M
articles.narrative: AV Management, a New York-based investment firm, has acquired the mixed-use property at 73-75 Sullivan St. in SoHo for $43.33 million from a long-term SoHo developer. The transaction was financed with a $21.6 million acquisition loan from Citizens Private Bank. AV Management noted that the acquisition represents an opportunity to purchase investment-grade assets at high yields by leveraging early-cycle market dynamics and the dislocation caused by recent interest rate hikes, with plans to hold the asset long-term for income.

data_points.address: 73-75 Sullivan St, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: Modern construction vintage with expansive layouts and long-term commercial tenancy.
data_points.project_notes: Acquired at an opportunistic basis to be held long-term as a permanent income vehicle.

articles.title: Multiverse Cantina Scum & Villainy Coming to Downtown Burbank
articles.narrative: Multiverse Cantina Scum & Villainy, an immersive fan experience bar and restaurant, is expanding its presence with a new 5,300-square-foot location in Downtown Burbank. This will be the second outpost for the popular concept, which originated in Hollywood. The new venue aims to cater to the entertainment industry's creative community and fans, offering a podcast studio, streaming capabilities, and a stage.

data_points.notable_features: Includes a podcast studio, streaming capabilities, and a stage.
data_points.project_notes: Second location for the concept, joining an inaugural location in Hollywood. The project will launch a Wefunder investment opportunity to create Founders Fleets.

articles.title: Family Office Collective Teams with Delshah, A.M. Properties on Midtown Office Condo
articles.narrative: REALM, a collective of 130 family offices, has partnered with Delshah Capital and A.M. Properties to acquire the 377,000-square-foot office condominium portion of CitySpire, located at 156 W 56th St. in Midtown Manhattan. The property, which is 98% occupied, was purchased at an 8.5% cap rate. The buyers noted strong fundamentals in premier Midtown assets, with leasing activity and occupancy outperforming the broader cautious capital markets for office properties.

data_points.address: 156 W 56th St, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: Comprises 24 floors within a 70-story tower, features renovated common areas and high-end finishes.
data_points.project_notes: Approximately $22 million in capital improvements have been made to the property, with no near-term capital expenditures required.

articles.market_intelligence: Manhattan office leasing activity reached pre-pandemic highs in late 2025.

articles.title: Stonelake Advancing Lamar Blvd. Mixed-Use Venture
articles.narrative: Stonelake is proceeding with a mixed-use development on South Lamar Boulevard, which will feature approximately 470 residential units for lease and 10,000 square feet of ground-floor food and beverage space. The project is planned as a six-story building, though recent zoning changes allow for construction up to 90 feet. Demolition of existing structures on the site is expected to precede a Q1 next year groundbreaking.

data_points.notable_features: Six-story building with potential for up to 90 feet in height due to recent zoning changes.
data_points.project_notes: Three existing properties, including commercial buildings and a duplex, will be demolished.

articles.title: Hudson Valley Property Group Expands California Presence with Elk Grove Acquisition
articles.narrative: Hudson Valley Property Group (HVPG) has acquired Mosa Apartment Homes, a newly constructed 387-unit affordable multifamily community in Elk Grove, CA, for $83.6 million, including assumed debt. This acquisition significantly expands HVPG's West Coast portfolio and its strategy to preserve affordability in newly delivered communities, complementing its work on repositioning legacy properties. The transaction involved three Low-Income Housing Tax Credit (LIHTC) partnerships, with HVPG acquiring the general partner interests.

data_points.notable_features: 14-building, garden-style family community with 100% of units covered by Low-Income Housing Tax Credit (LIHTC) regulatory agreements.
data_points.project_notes: Acquisition included assumption of debt and was structured across three LIHTC partnerships.

articles.title: Grocery REIT Considering Sale After Unsolicited Takeover Bid
articles.narrative: Slate Grocery REIT, a Canadian investor with a 15.2M SF portfolio of grocery-anchored properties across 23 U.S. states, is exploring strategic alternatives, including a potential sale, after receiving an unsolicited takeover bid from affiliates of its external manager, Slate Asset Management. The REIT's board has formed a special committee to evaluate options aimed at maximizing unitholder value, noting that the public market valuation may not fully reflect the underlying portfolio's worth. This move comes amidst a trend of public landlord mergers and acquisitions in the commercial real estate sector, driven by economic instability and a valuation gap between public and private real estate assets.

data_points.notable_features: Grocery-anchored portfolio with Kroger and Walmart as major tenants, nearly half of properties located in the Southeast.
data_points.project_notes: Portfolio valued at $2.4 billion across 23 states; the REIT's stock price of $16.78 equates to a market value of $992 million, trading at a more than 14% discount to its net asset value.

people.name: Andrea Stephen
people.title: Chair of Slate Grocery REIT's board

people.name: Marc Rouleau
people.title: Chair of the REIT’s special committee

articles.market_intelligence: The commercial real estate sector is experiencing a wave of public landlord mergers and acquisitions, with three acquisitions valued at over $1 billion occurring in Q1. This trend is driven by capital sources seeking refuge from economic instability and benefiting from the valuation gap between public and private real estate.

articles.title: Kayne Anderson Raises Record $5B For Latest Alternatives Fund
articles.narrative: Kayne Anderson Real Estate, the real estate private equity arm of Kayne Anderson Capital Advisors, has successfully closed its seventh fund, Kayne Anderson Real Estate Partners VII, with a record $5.12 billion in capital raised. This significantly exceeded its $3 billion target, demonstrating strong investor confidence in alternative real estate sectors. The fund will strategically focus on investments in medical office, senior housing, student housing, and light industrial assets, which the firm's CEO believes are poised for substantial growth driven by long-term demographic and e-commerce trends.

data_points.property_name: Kayne Anderson Real Estate Partners VII
data_points.notable_features: Fund focuses on medical office, senior housing, student housing, and light industrial assets, particularly last-mile logistics facilities.
data_points.project_notes: Fund launched in August 2024 and had attracted approximately $4 billion in cash and commitments by the end of 2025. This fundraise replaces the firm's previous record of $2.75 billion.

articles.title: Newmark Appoints Kyle Lutnick Chief Strategy Officer
NYC On The Verge Of A Construction Boom As Permit Filings Spike
articles.narrative: New building permit filings in New York City surged in Q1 2026, with developers seeking permits for 21.2M SF across 577 new projects, a 74% year-over-year increase. This spike is largely driven by multifamily proposals, accounting for nearly half of the projects and over 16,800 units, a 251% increase in units since 2008. The increase is a response to the city's housing crisis and Mayor Eric Adams' goal of 500,000 new housing units over the next decade. However, the Real Estate Board of New York (REBNY) notes that the majority of new developments are smaller, under 99 units, due to labor wage requirements associated with the 485-x tax incentive, which developers argue restricts larger-scale projects needed to address the crisis. While the rebound is significant, REBNY cautions it's not yet indicative of a sustained return to record highs, especially given the 'yo-yo' effect observed with the expiration of the 421-a tax incentive.

companies_people.label: OTHER
companies_people.firm_name: Real Estate Board of New York (REBNY)

articles.market_intelligence: Median monthly rents in Manhattan exceeded $5,000 for the first time in April. Nationally, apartment starts hit a 15-year low in Q1, contrasting with New York City's permit surge.
